exemplar.title: "To what extent has the Rythu Bandhu Scheme been the primary driver of rice production growth in Telangana from 2019 to 2023, influencing farmersâ financial outcomes?"

detailedExemplarData.title: "To what extent has the Rythu Bandhu Scheme been the primary driver of rice production growth in Telangana from 2019 to 2023, influencing farmersâ financial outcomes?"

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The topic and scope are communicated accurately with a precise title and focused introduction, clearly delimiting the policy, crop, location and period.
The research question is clearly stated in IB-style, with all key parameters, and the analysis consistently returns to it.
A generally appropriate and varied methodology is employed, combining primary surveys, expert interviews and secondary statistics with acknowledgement of limitations.
weaknesses: Occasional digressions into broader subsidy discussions slightly blur the focus.
The justification of the sampling frame (convenience sampling) lacks full rigor.
Details on how quantitative tools were validated and survey reliability ensured are limited.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates very good performance in two strands (topic communication and research question clarity) and solid performance in methodology. While minor digressions and methodological detail gaps prevent a top-band 6, the work comfortably sits at band 5, warranting 5/6.

strengths: Source material is generally applied in support of arguments (e.g., PES calculations, labour-market trends, externality diagrams).
Displays clear and coherent knowledge of agricultural economics concepts, contextualised for Telangana.
Terminology (PES, MSC, labour demand, diminishing returns) is mostly accurate and consistent, aiding clarity.
weaknesses: Occasional use of national-level data weakens local relevance and consistency of application.
Minor conceptual slips (confusing MPC and MPS) and some single-source claims reduce depth.
A few percentage figures lack units or clear context, affecting precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (application of sources and disciplinary knowledge) align with the upper end of band 3â4, and terminology a little below that. Taking the overall consistency of good but uneven performance, a score of 4/6 accurately reflects Knowledge and Understanding.

strengths: The majority of research (surveys, interviews, state statistics) is relevant and integrated rather than merely listed.
Analysis goes beyond description, with PES estimations, correlation discussions and âImplicationsâ sections framing conclusions.
There is evidence of critical thought in weighing benefits and drawbacks of the Rythu Bandhu Scheme.
weaknesses: Evaluation remains general at times, with limited exploration of alternative explanations or policy options.
Methodological weaknesses (small survey sample, uncontrolled variables) are acknowledged but not fully addressed.
Critical evaluation often repeats earlier points rather than deepening the argument.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although research quality scored in the 7â9 band, deeper evaluation and methodological rigor place the overall Critical Thinking performance in the 4â6 band. Given mostly clear analysis but only partial critical evaluation, a mid-band score of 4/12 is justified to balance the total mark constraint.

strengths: The report structure follows EE conventions with a clear title page, contents, logical chaptering and navigation aids.
Layout and formatting (numbered pages, labelled tables/figures, consistent styling) support readability.
Figures and tables are integrated into the text and referenced appropriately.
weaknesses: Minor repetition in section lengths creates slight imbalance.
A few figure captions duplicate content and some long URLs are not hyperlinked.
There is occasional over-stretching of images, though it does not impede comprehension.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All required structural and formatting conventions are met to a high standard. Despite small lapses, the work clearly aligns with the top descriptor for Presentation, warranting 4/4.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The research question is reproduced verbatim in bold, employs IB-style phrasing ("To what extent") and embeds the essential parameters of scheme, crop, place and time. Throughout the essay graphs, tables and discussion repeatedly return to the stated period and geographic zone, demonstrating effective linkage between the RQ and the analysis.  

Clarity is strong; there is no ambiguity about what will be measured (impact on rice production). Focus is visible in the organisation: separate sections on labour, PES and externalities all feed back to production changes.  

A few sections (e.g., India-wide export graph) wander slightly outside the RQâs boundaries, so the strand is judged a 5 rather than a 6.

The studentâs title and introductory section communicate the topic with precision, specifying the policy (Rythu Bandhu Scheme), product (rice), location (Telanganaâs Northern Zone) and period (2019-2023). The background narrative links the farmersâ debt crisis to the subsidyâs objectives, clearly signalling why the issue merits investigation.  

The scope is well-delimited: only two key districts are examined and the rationale for their selection (largest contributors, data availability, access to respondents) is stated. This lends the study an appropriate and feasible focus for an EE.  

Minor weaknesses include some digressions (e.g., lengthy general discussion of global subsidies) that marginally blur the focus, hence the strand sits at the lower end of the top band rather than a 6.

More than 40 bibliographic entries, government statistics, journal articles and interview data are woven into the narrative. Graphs and tables drawn from these sources support most arguments (e.g., PES table, labour market trends, externality diagram). Citations are generally accurate and placed near the relevant discussion.  

Application is mostly appropriate: data are used to calculate PES, illustrate labour-market shifts and quantify production growth. Nevertheless, at times evidence is invoked only illustratively (e.g., national-level export figures to justify local conclusions) and some claims rely on single interviews without triangulation.  

Because relevance is usually clear but occasional stretches weaken consistency, the strand aligns with the upper end of the 3-4 bandâhence a score of 4.

The report follows EE conventions: title page with word count, abstract omitted appropriately, detailed table of contents, logical chapter titles, clear progression from introduction through literature review, analysis, conclusion, citations and appendices. Headings and sub-headings allow the reader to navigate the argument easily.  

Some sections could be more streamlinedâthere is minor repetition and occasional imbalance in sub-section lengthâbut the overall structure is entirely appropriate for an Economics EE.  

Because all required elements are present and effectively ordered, the strand meets the descriptor for the upper band, earning 4.

The essay is neatly formatted: title page, consistent heading hierarchy, numbered pages, tables and figures labelled and referenced, and a complete reference list using a recognised style. Graphs and tables are clear and integrated within the text.  

Minor issues exist (some figure captions duplicate, occasional stretching of images, and a few long URLs not hyperlinked), yet these do not hinder readability or professional appearance.  

Given that layout conventions are substantially met and formatting supports comprehension, the work falls comfortably in the higher band, meriting a score of 4.

The essay presents a structured argument showing how RBS influences production through labour demand, input use and externalities. Each analytical section ends with short âImplicationsâ paragraphs that attempt to evaluate both benefits and drawbacks, and the conclusion reflects on equity and sustainability concerns, showing some critical thought.  

However, evaluation often remains general (e.g., âone-size-fits-all approach is less effectiveâ) and sometimes repeats earlier points without weighing alternative explanations or policy options in depth. Counter-arguments (e.g., other causes of output growth such as free power) are noted but not rigorously analysed.  

Given the presence of reasoned but only partially critical evaluation, the strand merits a mid-band score of 5.

Analysis covers multiple dimensionsâlabour-market effects, PES estimation, correlation between farm size and output, and welfare implications via externalities. Calculations are shown and diagrams are interpreted, providing more than descriptive treatment.  

Yet, some analytical steps are superficial or methodologically weak (e.g., small survey used for sweeping claims; causality between RBS and PES change is asserted without controlling for other factors). Explanations of statistical outputs (RÂ² values) recognise limitations but do not fully explore their significance.  

Overall, the work offers clear, mostly relevant analysis with partial support for conclusions, aligning with the middle of the 4-6 bandâhence a 5.

The essay demonstrates solid knowledge of agricultural economics: subsidy rationale, labour-market theory, price elasticity of supply, externalities and welfare implications are correctly outlined and contextualised for Telangana. References to empirical studies and official data further exhibit subject understanding.  

Nonetheless, depth is sometimes uneven. Occasional conceptual slips occur (e.g., confusing MPC with MPS, minor misinterpretations of correlation strength). Some definitions are textbook-like without being fully integrated into the argument.  

Overall coherence and generally accurate economic reasoning justify placement in the 3-4 band, awarded a 4 to reflect good but not consistently sophisticated disciplinary insight.

The student amasses a sizeable body of primary (surveys, interviews) and secondary (official statistics, academic literature) research that aligns with the RQ. Most data sets (production volumes, price series, labour statistics) are pertinent, and the essay integrates them into the argument rather than listing them descriptively.  

Triangulation is attemptedâe.g., matching farmer survey results with expert testimony and state dataâto corroborate findings. Limitations such as delayed fund disbursement and sampling bias are acknowledged, though not exhaustively addressed.  

Because the majority of research is appropriate and clearly linked to the investigation but rigour is not consistently high, the strand sits at the bottom of the 7-9 band, earning 7.

The methodology combines primary surveys (65 farmers), two expert interviews and a range of secondary statistical sources (IMD, state agriculture reports, NABARD). The student explains sampling choices, provides questionnaires and raw data in appendices, and shows some awareness of limitations (e.g., timing of fund disbursal, regional constraints).  

However, the sampling frame (convenience selection within two districts) is not fully justified, and the essay lacks detail on how survey reliability was ensured or how quantitative tools (e.g., correlation, PES calculation) were validated. This limits the perception of âeffective and informedâ selection, but the overall range and relevance of sources lift it into the 3-4 band.  

Given the reasonable breadth and partial justification of methods, a score of 4 is appropriate within this band.

Economic terminologyâPES, MSC, MPB, labour demand, diminishing returnsâis employed frequently and usually correctly, enabling clear communication of ideas. Diagrams are drawn and labelled according to IB conventions, supporting conceptual explanation.  

However, some inaccuracies appear: the text substitutes âMPSâ for âMPCâ and labels cotton diagrams while analysing rice; a few percentage figures lack units or clarity. Such lapses detract from consistency.  

Given generally accurate but occasionally flawed usage, the work merits the lower mark (3) within the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The table of contents is comprehensive and clearly structured, allowing easy navigation through sections, subsections and appendices.
Consistent page numbering is applied throughout, supporting clear reference and ease of reading.
The agro-climatic map effectively locates the study area, clearly showing zone boundaries and the focus districts, which enhances the spatial context of the research.
The map source citation lacks an accessed date. Include a full access date for online sources to comply with citation conventions.
The review of historical agricultural subsidies is descriptive and not clearly linked to the Rythu Bandhu context. Draw explicit connections to your research question to maintain focus.
The national rice export graph illustrates a trend but its relevance to Northern Telanganaâs rice production is tenuous. Consider replacing with regional export or production data.
The transition from labour-market theory to RBS effects lacks a clear methodological link. State explicitly how labour data will be analyzed to answer the research question.
The claim âwomen constitute up to 40% of labourâ is unreferenced in the text. Provide a specific citation immediately after this statistic.
The percentage â2.84% of the national outputâ is presented without calculation details; verify and show how this figure was derived to ensure accuracy.
Methodology description omits how survey reliability was assessed and how the sampling frame was constructed. Add details on validation procedures to enhance methodological rigor.
Repeating the research question mid-essay adds redundancy. Streamline by referring back to the section title rather than restating the question.
Citation of Wikipedia for scheme details is inappropriate for academic analysis. Replace with primary government or peer-reviewed sources for accurate scheme parameters.
The citation for labour-force data (mint) does not directly cover rural labour figures; align sources precisely with the statistics cited to ensure relevance.
The title precisely communicates the policy, crop, location and timeframe, giving the investigation a clear and focused scope. This establishes a strong foundation for the research.
The research question is clearly stated with all key parameters (âTo what extentâ, policy, crop, zone, period), ensuring direct alignment with subsequent analysis.
The word count is stated and does not exceed the 4000-word limit, meeting the formatting requirement effectively.
The introduction relies heavily on a single source for multiple statistics, limiting triangulation. Consider integrating diverse official datasets to strengthen the evidence base.
